text: suprapubic single-port ileocecal resection for complicated Crohn's disease. This is a case of a twenty-three years old female initially diagnosed with Crohn's disease at the age of twenty. She presented with complicated Crohn's disease located at the terminal ilium. An MRI enterography performed during the preoperative workup revealed a large pelvic mass involving the last ilial loop with a severe stenosis of the terminal ilium. The disease segment showed a maximum wall thickness of twelve millimeters.

suprapubic single-port ileocecal resection for complicated Crohn's disease. This is a case of a twenty-three years old female initially diagnosed with Crohn's disease at the age of twenty. She presented with complicated Crohn's disease located at the terminal ilium. An MRI enterography performed during the preoperative workup revealed a large pelvic mass involving the last ilial loop with a severe stenosis of the terminal ilium. The disease segment showed a maximum wall thickness of twelve millimeters.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy, and a 5 cm suprapubic incision is made. A wound protector retractor is placed, followed by a glove port with three standard trocars. The surgeon mobilizes the phlogistic mass from the retroperitoneum and inspects the diseased ileal loop. The cecum and ascending colon are mobilized from lateral to medial, preserving retroperitoneal structures. Blunt dissection is performed on the avascular plane, and an energy device is used to transect the paracolic gutter.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy, and a 5 cm suprapubic incision is made. A wound protector retractor is placed, followed by a glove port with three standard trocars. The surgeon mobilizes the phlogistic mass from the retroperitoneum and inspects the diseased ileal loop. The cecum and ascending colon are mobilized using a lateral to medial technique, preserving retroperitoneal structures. Blunt dissection is performed on the avascular plane, and an energy device is used to transect the paracolic gutter.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the hepatic flexure and proximal transverse colon, allowing the ileal and colonic stumps to be exteriorized through the wound. The mesocolon, ileocolic vessels, and mesentery of the distal ileum are sealed and sectioned intracorporeally using an energy device. The last ileal loop is divided with a laparoscopic linear stapler on a disease-free area, followed by the transaction of the ascending colon with a linear stapler.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the hepatic flexure and proximal transverse colon, allowing the exteriorization of the ileal and colonic stump through the wound. The mesocolon, ileocolic vessels, and mesentery of the distal ileum are sealed and sectioned intracorporeally using an energy device. The last ileal loop is divided using a laparoscopic linear stapler on a disease-free area, followed by the transaction of the ascending colon with a linear stapler.

The surgeon completes the exploration of the bowel, confirming no additional strictures or disease localization. The surgical specimen is extracted, and intestinal continuity is restored using a side-to-side staple ileocolonic anastomosis. The specimen is inspected on the back table.

The advantages of this technique include a direct surgical access to the pelvic mass, a reduction of the risk of postoperative hernia, and a better cosmetic outcome especially valuable for young Crohn's disease patients.
